topic: Someone discovers their recipe has become famous under another name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The aroma of burnt sugar and vanilla still clung to the kitchen air, a phantom scent that made my skin prickle. I meticulously cleaned every whisk, every bowl, as if scrubbing away some invisible residue. It had started subtly—a few extra orders, a mention in a glossy food magazine I hadn't even looked for. Now, the whispers followed me. "The secret behind the cloud-nine cake," people murmured at the coffee shop, eyes lingering on my apron. I checked the online mentions every ten minutes, my thumb hovering over the screen like a nervous tic. Every comment felt like an accusation, every positive review a carefully placed trap. I started locking the pantry, double-checking the locks, convinced someone was watching, waiting for me to slip up and reveal the true source of this sudden, unwanted acclaim.

[story 2]

My hands trembled as I reread the ingredient list for the sourdough starter. It was supposed to be just a simple combination of flour, water, and time. But then I saw it—a subtle alteration, a specific type of wild yeast I hadn't used before. A jolt of ice shot through my chest. Someone had taken my creation, my painstaking, private alchemy, and rebranded it. I paced the length of my living room, the floorboards groaning under my frantic steps. I kept glancing at the window, half-expecting to see a familiar face lurking in the shadows, someone who knew too much about the delicate balance of my baking. I started speaking in low, clipped tones, constantly scanning the periphery, certain that the fame was a carefully constructed illusion designed to expose some hidden vulnerability.

[story 3]

The phone buzzed incessantly, a relentless series of notifications demanding my attention. Each ring sounded like an intrusion, a signal that the facade was cracking. I stared at the screen, the bright light reflecting off my wide, strained eyes. They were talking about 'The Golden Crumb,' a name that sounded vaguely familiar, yet utterly alien to my own humble beginnings. I slammed the phone down, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet apartment. I began rearranging the spice rack, meticulously aligning the jars, a ritualistic attempt to impose order on a world that felt suddenly chaotic and infiltrated. If they knew what I had done, if they knew the true origins of this widespread adoration, then I needed to disappear. I started packing small bags, my movements jerky and hurried, preparing for a flight I wasn't sure I wanted to take, terrified of being traced back to the source.
